text: Minerals play a dual role in soil and sediment biogeochemistry by both inhibiting and prolonging extracellular enzyme activity through surface adsorption. While the oxygenation of mineral-bound Fe(II) is known to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), the impact of this process on the functional lifespan and catalytic efficiency of extracellular enzymes remains poorly understood. In this study, we investigated how the oxidation of mineral-bound Fe(II) affects the hydrolytic activity of the cellulose-degrading enzyme β-glucosidase (BG) across two reduced clay minerals (nontronite and montmorillonite) and one iron oxide (magnetite) at pH 5 and 7. Under anoxic conditions, BG adsorption onto mineral surfaces initially reduced its catalytic activity but extended its overall functional persistence. Conversely, exposure to oxygen triggered ROS production, where the concentration of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) correlated directly with the degree of structural Fe(II) oxidation. This ROS generation led to a significant loss of BG activity and a shortened lifespan driven by catastrophic conformational changes and structural decomposition. Our findings demonstrate that under oxic conditions, the inhibitory effects of ROS outweigh the protective benefits of mineral adsorption. This study reveals a previously unrecognized mechanism of enzyme inactivation, providing critical insights for modeling nutrient cycling and enzyme dynamics in redox-fluctuating environments.
